docs.title: Personal Cognitive Security System
docs.text: A personal practice framework for cognitive resilience, happiness, and effective goal execution.

docs.title: What This Is
docs.text: Cognitive security at the individual level: protecting and optimizing how you think, feel, and act. Not institutional defense against disinformation — personal defense against the forces (internal and external) that erode clarity, happiness, and effectiveness.

docs.title: The Knowledge Base
docs.text:
Understand the why behind each practice:

  1. Perception — Sleep, exercise, information diet. What you allow into your mind.
  2. Processing — CBT, metacognition, implementation intentions, pre-mortems. How you think.
  3. Output — Environmental design, reward protocols, gratitude, goal setting. What you do.
  4. Social Environment — Building your cognitive ecosystem. Who you surround yourself with.

docs.title: The Daily Practice
docs.text:
• Daily Checklist — Morning + day + evening routine

  • CBT Journal — Structured cognitive reappraisal template

  • Weekly Review — Sunday reflection and pattern extraction

docs.title: Tracking
docs.text: • Progress Tracker — Weekly scoring, monthly summaries, rollout plan

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
1. Read the wiki pages (20 min total)
  2. Start with the daily checklist — foundations only (sleep, exercise, no-phone, gratitude)
  3. Add one practice per week
  4. Do the weekly review every Sunday
  5. Don't try everything at once — the rollout plan in the tracker has the right sequence

docs.title: Evidence Base
docs.text: Every practice included has replicated research support. The wiki pages cite key studies. Nothing here is self-help fluff.

docs.title: Why This Is Different From Regular Journaling
docs.text:
Traditional journaling has no structure and no feedback loop. You write, it disappears into a file, nothing changes. That's why it felt like a dump.

CBT journaling is different:

  1. It's structured — you fill in specific columns, not free-write
  2. It's short — one entry takes 3-5 minutes, not 20
  3. It has a built-in insight — the "balanced thought" column IS the payoff. You don't need to review later.
  4. The benefit is in the writing, not the reading — the cognitive reappraisal happens as you write. Re-reading is optional.

You don't need to review these entries. The act of writing them rewires the thought pattern in real time. If you never look at them again, they still worked.

That said, weekly review of patterns (not individual entries) is valuable. See the weekly review template for how.

docs.title: The 4-Column Entry
docs.text: Fill this out once per day, or whenever something bothers you.

docs.title: Entry Date: ___
docs.text:
1. Situation (facts only — what a camera would record)

2. Automatic Thought (what went through your mind — the raw, unfiltered reaction)

3. Distortion Type (check which apply)

  • Catastrophizing — "This is a disaster"
  • All-or-nothing — "I completely failed"
  • Mind reading — "They think I'm..."
  • Overgeneralization — "This always happens"
  • Emotional reasoning — "I feel X, therefore X is true"
  • Should statements — "I should be..."
  • Discounting positives — "That doesn't count because..."
  • Personalization — "This is my fault" (when it isn't)
  • Fortune telling — "This will definitely go wrong"
  • Labeling — "I'm a failure" (instead of "this attempt failed")

4. Balanced Thought (not positive spin — accurate reassessment)

5. Outcome (optional — how do you feel after reappraising? 1-10)

Before reappraisal: /10 After reappraisal: /10

docs.title: Example 1: Work situation
docs.text: Column Content Situation Colleague didn't respond to my Slack message for 4 hours Automatic thought "They're ignoring me. They don't think my work is important." Distortion Mind reading + Personalization Balanced thought "They're probably busy. A 4-hour delay on Slack is normal. If it were urgent I'd call. Nothing here indicates disrespect."

docs.title: Example 2: Social situation
docs.text: Column Content Situation Went to a meetup, had surface-level conversations, left early Automatic thought "I'll never find my people here. Moving was a mistake." Distortion Fortune telling + All-or-nothing + Overgeneralization Balanced thought "One meetup is one data point. Surface-level conversation is normal at first meetings. It took years to build my previous network. I need more attempts, not a verdict."

docs.title: Example 3: Self-judgment
docs.text: Column Content Situation Browsed social media for 45 minutes instead of doing deep work Automatic thought "I have no discipline. I'll never stick to this system." Distortion Labeling + Fortune telling + All-or-nothing Balanced thought "I slipped once. That's information, not identity. What was the trigger? I was tired and didn't take a break after the last focus block. Next time: mandatory break after 90 min."

docs.title: Pattern Tracking (Weekly)
docs.text:
At your weekly review, scan this week's entries and note:

  1. Most common distortion type: ___ (This is your cognitive blind spot — the bias your brain defaults to)

  2. Most common trigger situation: ___ (This is where your defenses are weakest)

  3. Did reappraisals feel accurate? Yes / Somewhat / Forced (If forced, the balanced thoughts might still be too "positive spin" — aim for truly accurate, even if uncomfortable)

docs.title: Daily Checklist
docs.text: Print this or copy into your tracking tool. Check off as you go.

docs.title: Morning Block (15 min total)
docs.text:
• No phone for first 30 min after waking

  • 3 intentions written (before opening email/Slack/anything)

  • _____ because ___
  • _____ because ___
  • _____ because ___

  • Exercise (30 min, or note what you'll do later today)

  • Meditation (10 min — sit, breath, notice, return)

docs.title: Deep Work Blocks
docs.text:
• Pre-work: Music ritual (1-2 songs), then silence/ambient for the block

  • Phone in another room during focus blocks

  • Block length: 60-90 min, then mandatory break

  • Post-block reward: Stand up + walk (NOT browsing). Write 3 sentences about what you accomplished.

docs.title: Metacognitive Check-ins (no time cost — just notice)
docs.text:
• Caught a thought pattern? Label it: planning / judging / worrying / craving / other

  • Emotional flag fired? Named the emotion before reacting? (even once counts)

  • Implementation intention held? Did your "If X, then Y" fire today?

docs.title: If a Difficult Moment Happened
docs.text:
• CBT reappraisal (5 min — use practice/cbt-journal.md template)
  • Situation: ___
  • Automatic thought: ___
  • Distortion type: ___
  • Balanced thought: ___

docs.title: Evening Block (5 min total)
docs.text:
• 3 specific gratitudes (what happened + why it mattered)
  • 3-line review:
  • What worked today? ___
  • What didn't? ___
  • What will I do differently tomorrow? ___

  • Tomorrow's top priority identified (just one)

  • Phone in another room by 10pm

  • Screens off 30 min before target sleep time

docs.title: Scoring (optional, for tracker)
docs.text:
Count your checks. Don't aim for 100% — aim for consistency.

Score Meaning 12+ Excellent day — full practice 8-11 Good day — core practices hit 4-7 Partial day — foundations covered 0-3 Reset day — no judgment, try again tomorrow

The goal is not perfection. The goal is that most days land in the 8+ range over time.

docs.title: Quick Reference: Implementation Intentions
docs.text:
Write your current ones here and update weekly:

  1. If __, then I will __
  2. If __, then I will __
  3. If __, then I will __
  4. If __, then I will __
  5. If __, then I will __

docs.title: Weekly Review Template
docs.text: Do this every Sunday. 15-20 minutes. This is where the compound interest happens.

docs.title: 1. Score Review
docs.text:
How many days this week scored 8+ on the daily checklist? ___/7

Trend vs. last week: Better / Same / Worse

docs.title: 2. Foundation Check
docs.text:
Foundation This week Notes Sleep (7-9 hrs, consistent?) ___/7 nights Exercise (30 min, moderate?) ___/5 sessions Meditation (10 min?) ___/7 days Morning no-phone window? ___/7 days

If any foundation < 50% compliance: That's this week's priority. Don't add new practices until foundations are solid.

docs.title: 3. CBT Pattern Review
docs.text:
Scan your CBT journal entries from this week:

  • Most common distortion: ___
  • Most common trigger: ___
  • Did balanced thoughts feel genuine? ___
  • Any recurring automatic thought showing up? ___

Action: Write one implementation intention targeting the most common trigger:

"If __, then I will __"

docs.title: 4. Deep Work Audit
docs.text:
• How many 60-90 min focus blocks this week? ___
  • Did you use the post-block reward protocol? ___
  • Any browsing/social media relapses after focus blocks? ___
  • Music during deep work: silence/ambient or lyrics? ___

docs.title: 5. Social Environment
docs.text:
• Meaningful conversations this week (intellectual + emotional depth): ___
  • Any new community attempts? ___
  • Maintained a remote connection? ___
  • Hosted or initiated anything? ___

docs.title: 6. Gratitude Audit
docs.text:
• Did evening gratitudes most days? ___/7
  • Were they specific (not generic)? ___
  • One gratitude that stands out from this week:

docs.title: 7. Pre-Mortem (if applicable)
docs.text:
Any major decision or project coming up next week?

If yes:

  • Assume it failed. Why? ___
  • Preventive action: ___

docs.title: 8. Values Alignment Check (monthly — do on first Sunday of month)
docs.text:
• Are my current goals still aligned with my values? ___
  • Am I spending time on things that matter to me? ___
  • Anything I'm doing out of obligation that I should drop or renegotiate? ___

docs.title: 9. Next Week's Focus
docs.text:
One thing to improve: ___

One thing to maintain: ___

Updated implementation intentions: 1. If __, then I will __ 2. If __, then I will __ 3. If __, then I will __

docs.title: The Point of This Review
docs.text: This review replaces the "review your journal" step you never did. Instead of re-reading entries, you extract patterns and convert them into next week's actions. The entries themselves are disposable. The patterns are the value.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
Score each area weekly (Sunday, during your weekly review). Use a simple 1-5 scale.

Score Meaning 1 Didn't practice at all 2 1-2 days 3 3-4 days 4 5-6 days 5 Every day

docs.title: Week 1: ___
docs.text:
Area Score Notes Sleep (7-9hr, consistent) /5 Exercise (30 min) /5 Meditation (10 min) /5 Morning no-phone /5 Intention setting /5 Deep work blocks /5 Post-block reward protocol /5 CBT journaling /5 Metacognitive labeling /5 Evening gratitude /5 Evening review /5 Social connection (quality) /5 TOTAL /60

Daily checklist avg score: ___ Highlight of the week: __ Biggest challenge: __

docs.title: Week 2: ___
docs.text:
Area Score Notes Sleep (7-9hr, consistent) /5 Exercise (30 min) /5 Meditation (10 min) /5 Morning no-phone /5 Intention setting /5 Deep work blocks /5 Post-block reward protocol /5 CBT journaling /5 Metacognitive labeling /5 Evening gratitude /5 Evening review /5 Social connection (quality) /5 TOTAL /60

Daily checklist avg score: ___ Highlight of the week: __ Biggest challenge: __

docs.title: Week 3: ___
docs.text:
Area Score Notes Sleep (7-9hr, consistent) /5 Exercise (30 min) /5 Meditation (10 min) /5 Morning no-phone /5 Intention setting /5 Deep work blocks /5 Post-block reward protocol /5 CBT journaling /5 Metacognitive labeling /5 Evening gratitude /5 Evening review /5 Social connection (quality) /5 TOTAL /60

Daily checklist avg score: ___ Highlight of the week: __ Biggest challenge: __

docs.title: Week 4: ___
docs.text:
Area Score Notes Sleep (7-9hr, consistent) /5 Exercise (30 min) /5 Meditation (10 min) /5 Morning no-phone /5 Intention setting /5 Deep work blocks /5 Post-block reward protocol /5 CBT journaling /5 Metacognitive labeling /5 Evening gratitude /5 Evening review /5 Social connection (quality) /5 TOTAL /60

Daily checklist avg score: ___ Highlight of the week: __ Biggest challenge: __

docs.title: Monthly Summary
docs.text:
Week Total /60 Trend 1 2 3 4

Month average: ___/60

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text: Range Meaning 45-60 Strong practice — system is working 30-44 Solid foundation — keep building 20-29 Partial adoption — focus on foundations first < 20 System isn't sticking — simplify. Pick 3 practices only.

docs.title: Monthly Reflection
docs.text:
• Which practice had the biggest positive impact? ___
  • Which practice do I resist most? ___
  • What pattern did CBT journaling reveal about my thinking? ___
  • Am I closer to the kind of person I want to be? Why/why not? ___

docs.title: Rollout Plan (Don't Try Everything At Once)
docs.text:
Week 1-2: Foundations only

  • Sleep protocol
  • Exercise
  • Morning no-phone
  • Evening gratitude

Week 3-4: Add Layer 2

  • Meditation
  • CBT journaling (one entry/day)
  • Intention setting

Week 5-6: Add Layer 3

  • Deep work blocks with reward protocol
  • Environmental design changes
  • Implementation intentions

Week 7+: Full system

  • All practices active
  • Weekly review
  • Social environment strategy

The rule: Don't add the next tier until the current tier scores 3+ on most items.

docs.title: Tool Recommendations
docs.text:
This tracker works as markdown. For better daily tracking and sharing:

Best fit: Notion

  • Import these markdown files directly (Notion supports md import)
  • Create a daily database with the checklist as properties
  • Dashboard view for weekly/monthly trends
  • Shareable via link with colleagues
  • Templates for repeating the daily checklist

Alternatives:

  • Obsidian — if you prefer local-first, private, and markdown-native

  • Apple Shortcuts + Health — for automating sleep/exercise tracking

  • Streaks app (iOS) — simple habit streak tracking with visual feedback

  • Loop Habit Tracker (Android) — open-source, graphs, no account needed

For the CBT journal specifically:

  • Paper works surprisingly well (slower = more processing time = better reappraisal)
  • Or a dedicated section in Notion with the 4-column template as a database

For feedback on how things are going:

  • Your weekly review IS the feedback mechanism
  • The tracker totals over time show trajectory
  • Monthly reflection questions surface qualitative insights
  • If you want external feedback: share your tracker with a trusted friend and review together monthly (combines social accountability + the "trusted advisor" practice)

docs.title: Layer 1: Perception — What Gets In
docs.text: Your cognitive security starts with what you allow into your mind. Garbage in, garbage out applies to thoughts as much as code.

docs.title: Sleep (Foundation — Non-Negotiable)
docs.text:
Sleep is not a Layer 1 practice — it's the foundation all three layers sit on.

What happens without it:

  • Emotional regulation collapses (amygdala reactivity increases ~60%)
  • You can't distinguish real threats from noise
  • Cognitive bias sensitivity spikes
  • Willpower and executive function degrade
  • Memory consolidation fails

The protocol:

  • 7-9 hours, consistent schedule (same wake time even on weekends)
  • Dark, cool room (18-20°C / 65-68°F)
  • No screens 30-60 min before bed (blue light suppresses melatonin)
  • No caffeine after 2pm (caffeine half-life is 5-6 hours)
  • If you wake up and can't sleep after 20 min, get up and do something boring in dim light, then return

Key research: Walker, "Why We Sleep" (2017); Krause et al. (2017) on sleep deprivation and emotional brain reactivity.

Priority: Do this first. Everything else degrades without it.

docs.title: CBT for Insomnia (CBT-I)
docs.text:
CBT-I is the first-line clinical treatment for insomnia — recommended over medication by both AASM and ACP guidelines. Effects are durable at 12-month follow-up. Sleep medication loses efficacy over time; CBT-I gets stronger.

The 5 components:

docs.title: 1. Sleep Restriction (most powerful component)
docs.text:
This is counterintuitive and uncomfortable, but it's the active ingredient.

  • Calculate your actual average sleep time (e.g., 5.5 hours)
  • Set a fixed wake time (e.g., 6:00 AM) — non-negotiable, even weekends
  • Only allow yourself in bed for that window (e.g., 12:30 AM – 6:00 AM)
  • Track sleep efficiency: (time asleep / time in bed) x 100
  • When efficiency hits 85%+, expand the window by 15 minutes
  • Repeat until you reach your target

The first 1-2 weeks feel terrible. That's the point — you're building sleep drive and consolidating fragmented sleep. It works.

docs.title: 2. Stimulus Control
docs.text:
The goal: your brain should associate bed with sleep, not with lying awake worrying.

  • Bed is for sleep only (no phone, no reading, no TV)
  • Go to bed only when sleepy (not just tired)
  • If awake for more than 15-20 minutes, get up
  • Go to another room, do something boring in dim light
  • Return only when sleepy again
  • No clock-watching (turn clocks away)

docs.title: 3. Cognitive Restructuring (CBT for sleep-specific thoughts)
docs.text:
Same technique as the CBT journal, but targeting sleep catastrophizing:

Automatic thought Balanced thought "I won't function tomorrow" "I've had bad nights before and still managed" "I need exactly 8 hours" "Sleep needs vary. Some nights less is fine." "My insomnia is permanent" "CBT-I works for most people in 3-4 weeks" "If I don't sleep now I'll ruin tomorrow" "One bad night doesn't ruin a day. The pressure to sleep is making it harder."

docs.title: 4. Sleep Hygiene (necessary but insufficient alone)
docs.text:
• Consistent wake time (the single most important habit)
  • Cool room (18-20°C / 65-68°F), dark, quiet
  • No caffeine 10+ hours before bed (not just after 2pm — adjust to your sensitivity)
  • No alcohol — it helps onset but destroys REM architecture and causes early waking
  • No screens 30-60 min before bed

docs.title: 5. Relaxation Training
docs.text:
Pick one and practice consistently, not just on bad nights:

  • Progressive muscle relaxation: Tense each muscle group 5 seconds, release 10 seconds, work from toes to head
  • Diaphragmatic breathing: 4 seconds in (belly rises), 7 seconds out. 5-10 minutes.
  • Body scan: Attention moves slowly from feet to head, noticing sensation without changing anything
  • Yoga Nidra: Guided "yogic sleep" practice. Limited but promising evidence. Works as a relaxation technique. 10-20 min recordings available on YouTube and Insight Timer. Good complement, not a replacement for sleep restriction.

CBT-I program structure: Typically 6-8 weeks. Effects appear by weeks 3-4. Can be self-guided.

Self-guided CBT-I resources:

  • Insomnia Coach (VA app, free) — self-guided 5-week CBT-I program
  • CBTI Coach (VA app, free) — companion app for tracking
  • "Say Good Night to Insomnia" by Gregg Jacobs — book-based CBT-I
  • "The Sleep Solution" by W. Chris Winter — practical and accessible

Key research: Trauer et al. (2015, Annals of Internal Medicine) — meta-analysis showing effect sizes d=0.8-1.0 for sleep onset latency. Comparable short-term to sleep medication, superior long-term.

docs.title: Sleep Supplements (What Actually Has Evidence)
docs.text:
Magnesium glycinate (recommended form):

  • 200-400mg elemental magnesium before bed
  • Glycinate form is best absorbed and the glycine itself has calming properties
  • Citrate is acceptable but more laxative effect
  • Oxide is poorly absorbed — skip it
  • Many people are mildly deficient, especially with modern diets

Glycine:

  • 3g before bed
  • Modest evidence for subjective sleep quality and next-day alertness
  • Key research: Yamadera et al. (2007); Bannai et al. (2012)

L-theanine:

  • 200mg
  • Reduces anxiety, may improve sleep quality indirectly
  • Evidence is modest but low risk

What doesn't work:

  • Melatonin for insomnia — effective for jet lag and circadian disorders, weak for primary insomnia. Most pills (5-10mg) are pharmacological doses. Physiological dose is 0.3-0.5mg.
  • Antihistamines (Benadryl/ZzzQuil) — tolerance develops within days, anticholinergic risks long-term
  • Alcohol — worst sleep "aid." Destroys REM sleep.
  • Staying in bed longer trying to sleep — actively makes insomnia worse (opposite of sleep restriction)
  • Sleep hygiene alone — necessary foundation but insufficient without the behavioral components (sleep restriction + stimulus control)

docs.title: Exercise (Infrastructure)
docs.text:
Not a nice-to-have. Effect size on mood rivals SSRIs in clinical trials.

What it does:

  • BDNF release — neurogenesis (literally grows new brain cells)
  • Cortisol regulation — better stress response
  • Improved executive function for 2-4 hours post-exercise
  • Sleep quality improvement (reinforcing cycle)

The protocol:

  • 30 min moderate intensity, 4-5x/week
  • "Moderate" = can talk but not sing
  • Morning exercise is optimal for mood + cognitive boost throughout day
  • Consistency matters more than intensity

Key research: Blumenthal et al. (2007) — exercise vs. sertraline for depression; Ratey, "Spark" (2008).

docs.title: Information Diet
docs.text:
The principle: Treat information sources like dependencies — audit regularly, remove what's unused or harmful.

Monthly curation sweep:

  • Unfollow accounts that trigger reactive emotions without teaching you anything
  • Mute or block without guilt — this is hygiene, not rudeness
  • Replace doom-scroll sources with one high-quality long-form source per interest area

Morning no-input window:

  • First 30-60 min after waking: no phone, no email, no news
  • Your first thoughts set the cognitive frame for the day
  • Other people's priorities will hijack this window if you let them

Emotional flag protocol:

  • When content makes you angry, anxious, or fearful: pause 10 seconds
  • Name the emotion: "I notice I'm feeling angry"
  • This activates prefrontal cortex and dampens amygdala response
  • Then decide: is this worth my cognitive resources?

Key research: Lieberman et al. (2007) — affect labeling reduces amygdala activation; Newport, "Digital Minimalism" (2019).

docs.title: How These Compound
docs.text:
Good sleep --> better emotional regulation --> better information filtering
     |
Exercise --> better mood baseline --> less vulnerability to manipulation
     |
Clean inputs --> clearer thinking --> better decisions --> more energy for sleep/exercise

Each practice reinforces the others. Start with sleep, add exercise, then refine your information diet.

docs.title: Layer 2: Processing — How You Think
docs.text: Layer 1 controls what gets in. This layer upgrades the machinery that processes it.

docs.title: Cognitive Reappraisal (CBT Core Technique) — #1 Priority
docs.text:
The single most evidence-backed cognitive intervention. Not positive thinking — accurate re-evaluation.

What it is: Your brain generates "automatic thoughts" in response to events. Most are distorted. CBT teaches you to catch and correct them in real time.

Common distortions to watch for:

  • Catastrophizing: "This is a disaster" (Is it really? What's the actual worst case?)

  • All-or-nothing: "I completely failed" (Did you? Or did one part not work?)

  • Mind reading: "They think I'm incompetent" (Evidence? Or assumption?)

  • Overgeneralization: "This always happens" (Always? Or this time?)

  • Emotional reasoning: "I feel stupid, therefore I am stupid" (Feelings aren't facts)

  • Should statements: "I should be further along" (Says who? By what standard?)

  • Discounting positives: "That success doesn't count because..." (Why not?)

The 4-column technique (see practice/cbt-journal.md for template):

Column What to write Situation What happened? (facts only, no interpretation) Automatic thought What went through my mind? Distortion Which pattern above does this match? Balanced thought What's a more accurate way to see this?

Example:

  • Situation: "Colleague didn't respond to my message for 3 hours"
  • Automatic thought: "They're ignoring me, they don't respect my work"
  • Distortion: Mind reading
  • Balanced thought: "They might be busy. A 3-hour delay is normal. My message didn't require urgency."

Why this works: Forces System 2 (deliberate, analytical) to override System 1 (fast, reactive). Over time, the automatic thoughts themselves change.

Key research: Beck (1979) — foundational CBT; Hofmann et al. (2012) — meta-analysis of 269 studies confirming efficacy across anxiety, depression, anger, stress.

Practice: 5 minutes/day. One reappraisal. Use the template in practice/cbt-journal.md.

docs.title: Metacognitive Awareness — #2 Priority
docs.text:
Different from thinking better. This is awareness of thinking itself.

The distinction:

  • Cognitive: "I'm worried about the presentation"
  • Metacognitive: "I notice I'm worrying about the presentation"

That shift — from inside the thought to observing the thought — is the core skill. You can't fix what you can't see.

How to develop it:

docs.title: Formal practice: Meditation (10 min/day)
docs.text:
Not spiritual. Not relaxation. Cognitive training.

The protocol:

  1. Sit. Set timer for 10 min.
  2. Attention on breath (sensation at nostrils or belly).
  3. Mind wanders. Notice it wandered. Return to breath.
  4. That's it. The "noticing" IS the training. Wandering is not failure.

What this trains:

  • The gap between stimulus and response
  • Ability to observe thoughts without being captured by them
  • Emotional regulation (you see emotions arising before they hijack behavior)
  • Attentional control (the muscle that makes deep work possible)

Key research: Brewer et al. (2011) — meditation reduces default mode network activity; Goyal et al. (2014) — meta-analysis showing moderate effect size for anxiety, depression, pain.

docs.title: Informal practice: Thought labeling throughout the day
docs.text:
• "Planning" (when you catch yourself planning)
  • "Judging" (when you catch yourself judging)
  • "Worrying" (when you catch yourself worrying)
  • "Craving" (when you catch yourself wanting distraction)

No judgment on the label. Just label and return to what you were doing.

docs.title: Mantra repetition: Real-time thought displacement
docs.text:
Meditation trains you to observe thoughts. Mantra displaces them. Different mechanism, complementary tool.

How it works mechanistically:

Repeating a phrase (e.g., "Amitofou") occupies the verbal processing channel — the same channel that generates rumination, worry, and mental chatter. You can't catastrophize and chant simultaneously. It's a hard interrupt on default mode network activity.

When to use it:

  • Transition ritual — 1-2 minutes between activities to clear cognitive residue from the last task. Better than jumping straight from one context to the next.
  • Distraction interrupt — when you notice the urge to browse/scroll, 30 seconds of mantra breaks the craving loop.
  • Walking practice — sync with steps. Turns commuting into attention training without needing to sit.
  • Pre-sleep — replaces the mental replay/planning loop that keeps people awake.

Implementation intention: "If I feel the urge to check my phone during deep work, I will repeat Amitofou 10 times before deciding what to do."

How it fits with the other tools:

Tool Function Meditation Train awareness of thoughts (gym training) CBT journaling Correct distorted thoughts (surgery) Mantra Interrupt/displace distorted thoughts in real time (first aid)

Meditation builds the skill. CBT fixes the patterns. Mantra is portable, immediate, no setup required.

Key research: Bernardi et al. (2001, BMJ) — repetitive prayer/mantra slows respiration to ~6 breaths/min, maximizing heart rate variability and baroreflex sensitivity. Benson's "relaxation response" research: any repetitive word/phrase triggers parasympathetic activation. The specific word matters less than the repetition + intention.

docs.title: Implementation Intentions — #3 Priority
docs.text:
Peter Gollwitzer's research: "If X, then Y" statements roughly double follow-through probability.

Format: "If [situation], then I will [specific action]"

Examples:

  • "If I feel the urge to check social media during work, I will take 3 breaths and return to the task"
  • "If I finish a deep work block, I will stand up and leave my desk immediately"
  • "If someone says something that triggers me, I will pause and label the emotion before responding"
  • "If it's 10pm, I will put my phone in another room"

Why this works: Pre-loads the decision. When the trigger happens, you don't need willpower — the response is already decided. Offloads cognitive effort from the moment of temptation to a calm planning moment.

Key research: Gollwitzer (1999); meta-analysis by Gollwitzer & Sheeran (2006) — d = 0.65 effect size across 94 studies.

Practice: Write 3-5 implementation intentions for your current challenges. Revise weekly.

docs.title: Post-Deep-Work Recovery: What to Do and Why
docs.text:
Deep work depletes three things:

  1. Directed attention — the prefrontal cortex resource that holds focus. Finite. Depletes with use.
  2. Decision-making energy — decision fatigue is real even if ego depletion is debated.
  3. Physical tension — hours of stillness accumulates muscular and postural strain.

Best recovery restores all three simultaneously. Worst recovery (browsing, social media) depletes #1 further while pretending to help.

docs.title: Recovery Activities Ranked
docs.text:
Tier 1: Full restoration (restores all three)

Activity Why it's top tier Walking outdoors (nature if possible) Attention Restoration Theory (Kaplan, 1995): nature engages "soft fascination" — involuntary attention that lets directed attention recover. Movement, no decisions, fresh air. Even 10 min in a park measurably restores attention. Table tennis / sport with a partner Flow state on demand. Complex motor coordination occupies attention differently — active recovery. Social component. Unpredictable (no two rallies same) keeps you present. Movement clears physical tension. Swimming / cold shower Autonomic nervous system reset. Cold exposure triggers norepinephrine release (alertness without anxiety). Full-body sensory input pulls you completely out of cognitive mode.

Tier 2: Strong restoration (restores 2 of 3)

Activity Why it's strong Mantra walking (combined) Walking + mantra = physical recovery + verbal channel cleared of work residue. Sync mantra to steps. 5-10 min. Stretching / yoga flow Physical tension release + body awareness pulls attention away from cognitive mode. 5-10 min is enough. Playing music (instrument) Motor + auditory + creative — completely different brain networks. Excellent if you play. Juggling / physical skill practice Motor learning occupies attention restoratively. 5 min of juggling is surprisingly good. Cooking something simple Sensory (smell, touch, taste) + light motor + small creative decisions.

Tier 3: Moderate restoration (restores 1 of 3)

Activity Limitation Tea/coffee ritual (mindful) Sensory + ritual, but no movement Brief conversation Social recovery, but work topics don't clear context Drawing / doodling Creative channel switch, but still sitting

Tier 4: Fake restoration (avoid)

Activity Why it fails Browsing internet Depletes same directed attention you just used Social media Dopamine hit then crash — worse than before YouTube Passive consumption, no restoration News Emotional load + directed attention cost

docs.title: Post-Deep-Work Decision Tree
docs.text:
After finishing a deep work block:

1. Stand up immediately (don't "check one thing" first)
2. Is someone available for table tennis / sport?
   --> YES: play for 10-15 min
   --> NO: go to step 3
3. Weather OK for walking?
   --> YES: 10 min walk outside, mantra synced to steps
   --> NO: go to step 4
4. Indoor fallback:
   --> 5 min stretching + 2 min mantra standing

docs.title: Post-Deep-Work Implementation Intentions
docs.text:
1. "When I close my laptop / finish a focus block, I will stand up and leave my desk immediately."
  2. "If someone is available for table tennis, I will play for 10-15 minutes."
  3. "If no one is available, I will walk outside for 10 minutes with mantra."
  4. "If I can't go outside, I will stretch for 5 minutes and chant for 2 minutes."
  5. "I will not open a browser or check my phone until after the recovery activity."

Rule #5 is critical. The moment between finishing deep work and starting recovery is the vulnerability window. That's when "let me just check one thing" happens. The implementation intention pre-loads the decision so you don't need willpower in that moment.

docs.title: Pre-Mortem Analysis
docs.text:
Before important decisions or projects:

  1. Assume the project/decision has failed
  2. Write down every reason it might have failed
  3. For each reason, identify a preventive action
  4. Implement the preventive actions

Why this works: Overcomes optimism bias. Your brain naturally suppresses failure scenarios when you're excited about something. The pre-mortem gives failure scenarios permission to surface.

When to use: Major decisions, project kickoffs, important conversations, career moves.

Key research: Klein (2007) — prospective hindsight increases ability to identify reasons for future outcomes by 30%.

docs.title: Belief Auditing
docs.text:
Monthly practice:

  1. Pick one belief you hold strongly
  2. Write the strongest argument against it (steel-man)
  3. Ask: "What evidence would change my mind?"
  4. If no evidence could change your mind, that's a red flag — it's an identity attachment, not a belief

The test: Can you articulate the opposing view well enough that someone who holds it would say "yes, that's my position"? If not, you don't understand it well enough to reject it.

docs.title: Layer 3: Output — What You Do
docs.text: Perception and processing mean nothing without action. This layer turns clarity into execution and sustained happiness.

docs.title: Environmental Design — #1 Priority
docs.text:
The cheat code. Remove friction from good behaviors, add friction to bad ones. Environment beats willpower almost every time.

The principle: You don't rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems.

High-impact defaults:

  • Phone in another room during deep work (not face-down on desk — in another room)
  • No social media apps on phone home screen (bury them in folders or delete entirely)
  • Browser: use separate profiles for work and personal (no tabs bleeding between contexts)
  • Gym clothes laid out the night before
  • Healthy food visible and at arm's reach; junk food requires effort to obtain
  • Desk cleared at end of day so morning you faces a clean workspace

The friction principle:

  • Good habits: reduce to zero steps (e.g., meditation cushion already out)
  • Bad habits: add steps (e.g., log out of social media after each use, so next visit requires password)

Key research: Wendy Wood, "Good Habits, Bad Habits" (2019); BJ Fogg, "Tiny Habits" (2019).

docs.title: The Post-Focus Reward Problem
docs.text:
Deep work depletes directed attention. Your brain craves easy dopamine to recover. Browsing/social media provides it — but it's a trap because it further depletes attention and trains your brain to expect cheap rewards.

Better reward substitutions (matched to the dopamine need):

Instead of... Try... Why it works Browsing internet 5-min walk outside Movement + nature restore attention (Attention Restoration Theory). Genuine neurological recovery. Social media scroll Make tea/coffee mindfully Ritual + sensory engagement. Small pleasure without attention cost. YouTube rabbit hole 5 min of stretching Physical reward. Releases tension accumulated during focus. News checking Short conversation with someone Social reward is more satisfying and doesn't fragment attention. Random browsing Write 3 sentences about what you just accomplished Consolidates the win. Gives your brain closure on the focused block.

The music-while-working question:

Research is mixed but trends clear:

  • Music with lyrics: measurably hurts complex cognitive work (Perham & Currie, 2014)
  • Familiar instrumental music: neutral to slightly positive for routine tasks
  • Silence or nature sounds: best for deep/novel work
  • Music before work: boosts mood and can improve subsequent performance

Suggested protocol:

  • Use music as a pre-work ritual (1-2 songs to get into state)
  • Deep work: silence or nature sounds (rain, ambient)
  • Routine work: familiar instrumental OK
  • Reward block: music you love, guilt-free, for 5-10 min between focus blocks

Implementation intention: "When I finish a focus block, I will stand up, walk to [specific spot], and take 10 breaths before deciding what to do next."

docs.title: Gratitude Practice (Specific, Not Generic)
docs.text:
Strongest positive psychology intervention replicated across cultures.

The key: Specificity. "I'm grateful for my health" does almost nothing. "I'm grateful that my knee didn't hurt during today's run" rewires the brain.

Daily practice (evening, 2 min): Write 3 specific things. Each must answer: what specifically happened, and why did it matter to me?

  • Not: "Grateful for my job"
  • Yes: "Grateful that my manager gave me honest feedback on the proposal instead of polite avoidance — it made the final version much stronger"

Key research: Emmons & McCullough (2003) — measurable happiness increase in 3 weeks.

docs.title: Values-Aligned Goal Setting
docs.text:
Most goal failure isn't discipline failure — it's alignment failure.

The recursive "why" test: 1. State your goal 2. Ask "why do I want this?" 3. Ask "why" again on that answer 4. Repeat until you hit something that doesn't need justification

If you hit "because I should" or "because other people expect it" — that goal has weak alignment and will require constant willpower to pursue.

If you hit "because this is who I want to be" or "because this genuinely excites me" — that goal has strong alignment and will pull you forward.

Review quarterly: Are your active goals still aligned? Life changes. Goals should too.

docs.title: Intention Setting (Daily)
docs.text:
Morning protocol (2 min): 1. Write 3 priorities max for the day 2. For each: "This matters because [connection to values/goals]" 3. Write before opening email, Slack, or any input

This prevents reactive drift. Without it, you'll execute other people's agendas all day and feel productive but unfulfilled.

docs.title: Energy-Based Scheduling
docs.text:
Map your energy:

  • Track for one week: rate your focus/energy 1-5 at 9am, 12pm, 3pm, 6pm, 9pm
  • You'll see a pattern (most people peak 2-4 hrs after waking)

Protect peak hours:

  • Deep work only during peak hours
  • Meetings, email, admin during low-energy windows
  • Never schedule a meeting during your peak if you can avoid it

The compound effect: Right task + right energy level = 2-3x output vs. forcing deep work during a trough.

docs.title: Layer 4: Social Environment — Your Cognitive Ecosystem
docs.text:
This isn't a "nice to have" layer. Framingham Heart Study (20+ years, longitudinal): happiness, habits, and cognitive patterns spread through social networks up to 3 degrees of separation. You literally absorb the patterns of people around you.

Social environment is the highest-leverage perception filter that exists.

docs.title: The New City Problem
docs.text:
Moving to a new city means rebuilding your cognitive ecosystem from scratch. The challenge is specific: finding people who match your intellectual and emotional wavelength when the obvious community pathways (temples, general meetups) lead to groups that don't fit for demographic or language reasons.

This is a solvable problem. It requires strategy, not luck.

docs.title: High-Signal Strategies for Finding Your People
1. Optimize for Conversation Quality, Not Activity Type
docs.text:
The mistake: joining activities you like and hoping the right people show up. The fix: join activities that select for the kind of thinking you want to be around.

High-selection-pressure activities:

  • Philosophy discussion groups (not lectures — discussions, where people have to think out loud)
  • Book clubs with serious reading lists (literary fiction, science, philosophy — not bestseller-of-the-month)
  • Rationalist/EA community (LessWrong meetups exist in many cities including Vancouver)
  • Hackathons, startup weekends, builder communities (selects for doers)
  • Graduate-level continuing education or audit courses at local universities
  • Toastmasters (specifically for impromptu speaking — reveals how people think)
  • Writing groups (people who write seriously tend to think seriously)

Why these work: They have an intellectual barrier to entry. The activity itself filters for the kind of mind you're looking for.

docs.title: 2. Host, Don't Just Attend
docs.text:
Fastest way to build a network: create the gathering you wish existed.

Low-effort, high-signal formats:

  • Monthly dinner (6-8 people, topic-based conversation): "I'm hosting a dinner to discuss [topic]. Interested?"
  • Walking conversations: invite one interesting person for a 1-hour walk. No agenda. Just think together.
  • "Interesting people" dinner chain: find one person you click with, ask them to bring one person you'd find interesting

Why hosting works: You control the selection criteria. You set the intellectual tone. And people appreciate someone who organizes — it's scarce and valuable.

docs.title: 3. Use Digital to Find Local
docs.text:
• Twitter/X: search for people in your city tweeting about topics you care about. DM them for coffee. Hit rate is surprisingly high.
  • Meetup.com: filter hard. Skip anything with 100+ attendees (too dilute). Target 8-20 person events.
  • Eventbrite: look for niche talks, not mainstream events
  • Discord/Slack communities: many have city-local channels

docs.title: 4. The Buddhist Community Specifically
docs.text:
The temple experience (one ethnic group, language barrier) is common. Alternatives:

  • Secular Buddhist groups: These tend to attract English-speaking, intellectually curious practitioners. Search for "secular Buddhist" or "insight meditation" groups — Western-adapted and discussion-heavy.

  • Zen centers: Especially those affiliated with Western Zen lineages. More diverse, English-primary, and tend to attract the intellectual/contemplative type.

  • Insight meditation communities (Spirit Rock / IMS lineage): Sophisticated, inquiry-oriented members.

The practice is the same. The community wrapper makes the difference.

docs.title: 5. The 3-Deep Rule
docs.text:
Don't evaluate a community by the first interaction. Give it 3 visits. First time you're processing logistics. Second time you're starting to see who's there. Third time you know if the intellectual and emotional wavelength matches.

But after 3 tries with no connection: move on. Don't force fit.

docs.title: Maintaining Your Previous Network
docs.text:
Connections from your previous city are still alive. Nurture them deliberately:

  • Monthly video calls with your closest intellectual peers
  • Share articles/ideas asynchronously (group chat, small email list)
  • Visit 1-2x/year for immersion

Remote relationships decay faster than you think. Schedule maintenance or they'll fade.

docs.title: The Deeper Principle
docs.text:
You need 3-5 people who: 1. Challenge your thinking (not yes-people) 2. Have permission to tell you when you're wrong 3. Are pursuing growth in their own life 4. You can be fully honest with

Quality over quantity. 3 deep connections > 30 surface-level meetup acquaintances.

Finding them takes 6-12 months of active effort in a new city. That's normal. Don't mistake the timeline for failure.
